questions.question: A train travels at a certain average speed for a distance of $540 \text{ km}$. If the speed had been $9 \text{ km/h}$ more, it would have taken $1 \text{ hour}$ less for the same journey. Form a quadratic equation to represent this situation and find the original average speed of the train.

steps: Let the original speed of the train be $x \text{ km/h}$.
The time taken to travel $540 \text{ km}$ at speed $x$ is $T_1 = \frac{540}{x} \text{ hours}$.
If the speed is increased by $9 \text{ km/h}$, the new speed is $(x+9) \text{ km/h}$.
The time taken at the new speed is $T_2 = \frac{540}{x+9} \text{ hours}$.
According to the problem, $T_1 - T_2 = 1$, so $\frac{540}{x} - \frac{540}{x+9} = 1$.
Simplify the equation to form the quadratic: $540(x+9) - 540x = x(x+9)$, leading to $x^2 + 9x - 4860 = 0$.
Solve the quadratic equation using the splitting the middle term method: $x^2 + 72x - 63x - 4860 = 0 \Rightarrow x(x+72) - 63(x+72) = 0$.

The roots are $x = 63$ (rejected since speed must be positive) is incorrect, re-evaluating: $x^2 + 9x - 4860 = 0$ gives $(x+72)(x-63)$ is wrong. Testing $45$: $45^2 + 9(45) = 2025 + 405 = 2430$ (too low). Re-solving $x^2 + 9x - 4860 = 0$: $x = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{81 - 4(1)(-4860)}}{2} = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{81 + 19440}}{2} = \frac{-9 \pm \sqrt{19521}}{2} = \frac{-9 \pm 139.7$ wait, discriminant is $19521$, $\sqrt{19521} = 139.7$ (not perfect square). Re-calculating: $540 \times 9 = 4860$. Factors of $4860$ with difference $9$ are $60$ and $72$? No, $60 \times 81 = 4860$. So $x^2 + 81x - 60x - 4860 = 0$. Roots are $60$ and $-81$. The speed is $60 \text{ km/h}$.
